metadata.evidence_text: A spear-phishing link automatically redirected the victim to execute a PowerShell Cobalt Strike Beacon.

procedure.action: automatically redirected the victim
procedure.purpose: to execute the beacon for initial access

metadata.evidence_text: A separate spear-phishing Word attachment created scheduled tasks (via a malicious macro) to pull further payloads from the C2 server.

procedure.action: created scheduled tasks
procedure.purpose: to pull further payloads from the C2 server

metadata.evidence_text: A VBScript loader then executed its commands, and its payload was first decoded from a base64/XOR blob.

procedure.action: executed its commands
procedure.purpose: to run the loader

metadata.evidence_text: A VBScript loader then executed its commands, and its payload was first decoded from a base64/XOR blob.

procedure.action: decoded
procedure.purpose: to reveal the payload

metadata.evidence_text: The decoded stage executed a PowerShell beacon in memory, completing the second delivery path into the same Cobalt Strike foothold.

procedure.action: executed a PowerShell beacon in memory
procedure.purpose: to complete the second delivery path into the same Cobalt Strike foothold

metadata.evidence_text: After the beacon executed, the attackers established persistence through Windows Registry Autorun keys.

procedure.action: established persistence through Windows Registry Autorun keys
procedure.purpose: to achieve persistence

metadata.evidence_text: They hid the persisted payloads using NTFS Alternate Data Streams.

procedure.action: hid the persisted payloads using NTFS Alternate Data Streams
procedure.purpose: to hide the payloads

metadata.evidence_text: They created and modified Windows Services to load the PowerShell beacon payloads.

procedure.action: created and modified Windows Services
procedure.purpose: to load the PowerShell beacon payloads

metadata.evidence_text: They also used a malicious Outlook backdoor macro for persistence by editing the LoadMacroProviderOnBoot value.

procedure.action: used a malicious Outlook backdoor macro for persistence by editing the LoadMacroProviderOnBoot value
procedure.purpose: to achieve persistence via Outlook

metadata.evidence_text: A second set of scheduled tasks (a GoogleUpdate task) was used to run the malicious loaders on a timer.

procedure.action: used a second set of scheduled tasks (a GoogleUpdate task)
procedure.purpose: to run the malicious loaders on a timer

metadata.evidence_text: These loader-launching mechanisms — the Wsearch service and the scheduled task — ultimately performed DLL side-loading, placing a fake msfte.dll and goopdate.dll that the legitimate Windows Search and GoogleUpdate binaries loaded.

procedure.actor: loader-launching mechanisms (Wsearch service and scheduled task)
procedure.action: performed DLL side-loading, placing a fake msfte.dll and goopdate.dll
procedure.purpose: to be loaded by legitimate Windows Search and GoogleUpdate binaries

metadata.evidence_text: The side-loaded loaders then used a regsvr32 COM-scriptlet to load additional Cobalt Strike payloads.

procedure.action: used a regsvr32 COM-scriptlet
procedure.purpose: to load additional Cobalt Strike payloads

metadata.evidence_text: For command and control, the fileless component used Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 profiles to impersonate Amazon and Google services through protocol impersonation.

procedure.action: used Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 profiles to impersonate Amazon and Google services
procedure.purpose: to evade detection through protocol impersonation

metadata.evidence_text: One C2 channel tunneled through DNS, relaying via Google and OpenDNS to reach the real C2 domain.

procedure.action: tunneled through DNS, relaying via Google and OpenDNS
procedure.purpose: to reach the real C2 domain

metadata.evidence_text: Another C2 channel was an Outlook mail backdoor that relayed the attackers' commands through Gmail.

procedure.action: relayed the attackers' commands through Gmail
procedure.purpose: to communicate with C2

metadata.evidence_text: After establishing C2, the attackers scanned the network for open ports and services.

procedure.action: scanned the network for open ports and services
procedure.purpose: to discover open ports and services

metadata.evidence_text: From that scanning they ran remote system discovery to enumerate the domain controllers.

procedure.action: ran remote system discovery to enumerate
procedure.purpose: to identify critical systems

metadata.evidence_text: They also gathered network configuration with ipconfig and network connections with netstat.

procedure.action: gathered network configuration with ipconfig
procedure.purpose: to discover network settings

metadata.evidence_text: They also gathered network configuration with ipconfig and network connections with netstat.

procedure.action: gathered network connections with netstat
procedure.purpose: to discover active connections

metadata.evidence_text: The reconnaissance led the attackers to dump credentials from LSASS using Mimikatz, obtaining NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets.

procedure.action: dumped credentials from LSASS using Mimikatz
procedure.purpose: to obtain NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets

metadata.evidence_text: With those credentials they performed pass-the-hash attacks.

procedure.action: performed pass-the-hash attacks
procedure.purpose: to authenticate without plaintext passwords

metadata.evidence_text: Separately, they also carried out pass-the-ticket attacks.

procedure.action: carried out pass-the-ticket attacks
procedure.purpose: to authenticate without plaintext passwords

metadata.evidence_text: Using the stolen authentication material they spread via SMB/Windows Admin Shares.

procedure.action: spread via SMB/Windows Admin Shares
procedure.purpose: to move laterally using stolen authentication material

metadata.evidence_text: They also abused Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) with the stolen credentials to deploy tools on remote machines.

procedure.action: abused Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) with the stolen credentials
procedure.purpose: to deploy tools on remote machines

text: A spear-phishing link automatically redirected the victim to execute a PowerShell Cobalt Strike Beacon. A separate spear-phishing Word attachment created scheduled tasks (via a malicious macro) to pull further payloads from the C2 server. A VBScript loader then executed its commands, and its payload was first decoded from a base64/XOR blob. The decoded stage executed a PowerShell beacon in memory, completing the second delivery path into the same Cobalt Strike foothold. After the beacon executed, the attackers established persistence through Windows Registry Autorun keys. They hid the persisted payloads using NTFS Alternate Data Streams. They created and modified Windows Services to load the PowerShell beacon payloads. They also used a malicious Outlook backdoor macro for persistence by editing the LoadMacroProviderOnBoot value. A second set of scheduled tasks (a GoogleUpdate task) was used to run the malicious loaders on a timer. These loader-launching mechanisms — the Wsearch service and the scheduled task — ultimately performed DLL side-loading, placing a fake msfte.dll and goopdate.dll that the legitimate Windows Search and GoogleUpdate binaries loaded. The side-loaded loaders then used a regsvr32 COM-scriptlet to load additional Cobalt Strike payloads. For command and control, the fileless component used Cobalt Strike Malleable C2 profiles to impersonate Amazon and Google services through protocol impersonation. One C2 channel tunneled through DNS, relaying via Google and OpenDNS to reach the real C2 domain. Another C2 channel was an Outlook mail backdoor that relayed the attackers' commands through Gmail. After establishing C2, the attackers scanned the network for open ports and services. From that scanning they ran remote system discovery to enumerate the domain controllers. They also gathered network configuration with ipconfig and network connections with netstat. The reconnaissance led the attackers to dump credentials from LSASS using Mimikatz, obtaining NTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets. With those credentials they performed pass-the-hash attacks. Separately, they also carried out pass-the-ticket attacks. Using the stolen authentication material they spread via SMB/Windows Admin Shares. They also abused Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) with the stolen credentials to deploy tools on remote machines.
